name: FIFA World Cup 2026 team analysis
attribution: Synthesised from the Guardian Football Weekly World Cup group previews (auto-caption transcripts). Concise editorial paraphrase.

MEX.narrative: Javier 'El Vasco' Aguirre's third World Cup in charge of El Tri, perennial group-stage qualifiers who slipped up in Qatar and now host with everything geared around home advantage.
MEX.key_players: A mostly home-based squad pulled into camp early; veteran keeper Guillermo Ochoa in for a sixth World Cup.
MEX.hinges_on: Leaning on altitude, heat and home advantage to win a kind group and stay in Mexico for the last 32.
notes: Play at altitude in Mexico City (2,240m) and Guadalajara, plus heat in Monterrey.
Arguably weaker on paper than past Mexican generations.
Domestic-based players are well rested and acclimatised.

RSA.narrative: Back at a World Cup for the first time since hosting in 2010, built around a strong Mamelodi Sundowns core fresh off an African Champions League win.
RSA.key_players: Heavy Sundowns contingent under Belgian coach Hugo Broos.
RSA.hinges_on: Surprising people by nicking a game in an open group.
notes: Arrived late in the US due to player visa issues.
Few players based abroad, so largely an unknown quantity.
Tipped by some to finish bottom of Group A.

KOR.narrative: Hong Myung-bo, the 2002 semi-final captain, leads a side a touch weaker than recent vintages chasing another knockout spot.
KOR.key_players: Son Heung-min (33), the talisman, though he scored only twice in MLS this year.
KOR.hinges_on: Getting something from an open group without needing to be brilliant.
notes: Already grumbling about playing the whole tournament at altitude.
Lost 4-0 to Ivory Coast and 1-0 to Australia in recent friendlies.

CZE.narrative: Miroslav Koubek's spirited, physical side came through the playoffs but lack ball-playing finesse.
CZE.key_players: Patrik Schick and Tomas Soucek; nearly half the squad play together at Slavia Prague.
CZE.hinges_on: Whether their cohesion can offset a brutal travel-and-heat schedule.
notes: Punishing draw: altitude vs Korea in Guadalajara, then 3,500km hops to Atlanta and Mexico City.
Based in New Jersey, making little effort to acclimatise.
Coach is 74.

CAN.narrative: Jesse Marsch's side, billed as the best Canadian team ever, want to top the group to stay in Vancouver.
CAN.key_players: Alphonso Davies (fitness a major doubt), Jonathan David (Juventus) and Tajon Buchanan.
CAN.hinges_on: Alphonso Davies' fitness and finally turning good play into goals.
notes: Davies may miss the first game or two.
First non-Canadian coach; signed an extension to 2030.
'Ted Lasso' positivity from Marsch.

BIH.narrative: Beat Italy and Wales in the playoffs to reach only their second World Cup as an independent nation.
BIH.key_players: 40-year-old Edin Dzeko still scoring; teenager Kerim Alajbegovic (Salzburg) caught the eye in the playoffs.
BIH.hinges_on: Finding runners to do Dzeko's running in the heat.
notes: Surprise playoff run, aided by an Italy red card.
Alajbegovic tipped to break out.

QAT.narrative: Hosts in 2022, back via the Asian fourth-round playoff after churning through three coaches in qualifying.
QAT.key_players: Akram Afif, off a title-winning Qatar Stars League season, under coach Julen Lopetegui.
QAT.hinges_on: Simply winning a game; their third group match is the realistic chance.
notes: Not big physically and could get bullied.
Lost 1-0 to Ireland in a recent friendly, mostly against 10 men.

SUI.narrative: Murat Yakin's experienced, deeply familiar core grinds out effective, low-mistake football.
SUI.key_players: Granit Xhaka (4th World Cup), Manuel Akanji and Dan Ndoye, with Breel Embolo up front.
SUI.hinges_on: Defensive solidity and taking their chances; they are often more durable than expected.
notes: Conceded just two goals in qualifying.
Shaqiri, Sommer and Schar have retired from international duty.
Short on a creative spark.

BRA.narrative: Carlo Ancelotti's five-time winners limp out of a poor qualifying campaign (lost six of 18) but have the talent to leave it behind.
BRA.key_players: Vinicius Jr, Raphinha, Bruno Guimaraes and Casemiro; Rayan (Bournemouth) made the cut.
BRA.hinges_on: Balancing an attack-stacked XI so the forwards actually track back.
notes: Neymar's omission and fitness saga threaten to overshadow.
Missing Rodrygo, Estevao and Militao to injury; Alisson had an injury-hit season.
Climate suits them more than most big nations.
Sometimes line up in a front-loaded 4-2-4.

MAR.narrative: 2022 semi-finalists under new boss Mohamed Ouahbi, the U20 World Cup-winning coach, who has promoted youngsters and plays more adventurously than the pragmatic Regragui.
MAR.key_players: Brahim Diaz, plus a canny, solid defense and a couple of game-changers.
MAR.hinges_on: Defensive solidity and patience in the heat; too good to be mere dark horses.
notes: Reached the semis in 2022.
Pundits split on whether more 'adventure' is wise in this climate.

HAI.narrative: First World Cup since 1974, qualifying amid a state of emergency and playing 'home' games in Curacao.
HAI.key_players: Jean-Ricner Bellegarde and goalkeeper Johny Placide.
HAI.hinges_on: Avoiding a heavy defeat in a group with Brazil and Morocco.
notes: Coach Sebastien Migne and most players have never set foot in Haiti.
In 1974 they ended Dino Zoff's 1,142-minute clean-sheet run before losing to Italy.

SCO.narrative: First World Cup since 1998 and a chance to escape a group for the first time ever, with everything riding on the opener vs Haiti.
SCO.key_players: A strong midfield and in-form Lawrence Shankland up top; lost Billy Gilmour to injury (Tyler Fletcher called up).
SCO.hinges_on: Beating Haiti by a two- or three-goal margin in game one.
notes: Goalkeeper and centre-back are problem areas (Craig Gordon is 43).
Experienced, highly motivated squad, many on a last World Cup chance.
The Tartan Army will travel in huge numbers.

USA.narrative: Hosts under Mauricio Pochettino, riding the home-soil belief they can reach the quarter-finals despite friction with their coach.
USA.key_players: Christian Pulisic, on fire in a recent friendly vs Senegal, in a poster-boy moment.
USA.hinges_on: Starting well and winning the group to open up the draw.
notes: Players reportedly dislike the coach, and vice versa.
A strong start tends to carry host nations a long way.

AUS.narrative: Tony Popovic's Socceroos arrive with rare optimism on the back of a solid crop of young talent.
AUS.key_players: Jordi Bos (Feyenoord, LWB), Alessandro Circati, Mohamed Toure and wildcard Nestory Irankunda.
AUS.hinges_on: Squeezing into the knockouts from an open group.
notes: Likely starter Riley McGree ruled out injured.
Christian Volpato switched allegiance from Italy and has never played for the Socceroos.

PAR.narrative: Gustavo Alfaro's counter-attacking side beat Brazil, Argentina and Uruguay in qualifying and fancy a knockout spot.
PAR.key_players: Miguel Almiron (ex-Newcastle), Ramon Sosa (Nottingham Forest) and centre-back Omar Alderete.
PAR.hinges_on: Finding goals; they don't score many.
notes: Happy to cede possession and strike on the break.
Manager nicknamed 'the lettuce'.

TUR.narrative: Vincenzo Montella's perennial dark-horse pick, with a favourable draw and quarter-final pedigree from Euro 2024.
TUR.key_players: Arda Guler and Kenan Yildiz, sparkling talent behind rugged defenders.
TUR.hinges_on: A kind group and their system clicking to win the group.
notes: Shipped six against Spain in qualifying, but otherwise in good form.
The eternal 'is it finally Turkey's year?' meme.

GER.narrative: Julian Nagelsmann's side will advance by format but again looks short of a true centre-forward.
GER.key_players: Florian Wirtz and Jamal Musiala (injury doubt); Kai Havertz likely as a false nine.
GER.hinges_on: Whether Havertz can lead the line and the big talents stay fit.
notes: Recalled 40-year-old Manuel Neuer, risking friction with Oliver Baumann.
Grim qualifying despite a 6-0 win over Slovakia.
Possible quarter-final vs France.

CUW.narrative: The smallest nation ever to qualify, an almost entirely Netherlands-raised squad led back by 78-year-old Dick Advocaat.
CUW.key_players: Tahith Chong (the only squad member born in Curacao) and the Bania brothers in midfield.
CUW.hinges_on: Sitting deep and hitting on the break; survival, realistically.
notes: Advocaat stepped down for family reasons, then was rehired weeks before the tournament.
Will be the oldest coach in World Cup history at 78.
Dramatic final-game qualification in Jamaica (a late penalty was VAR-overturned).

CIV.narrative: Emerse Fae's Ivory Coast finally land a group they ought to escape, riding momentum from a dramatic 2023 AFCON title.
CIV.key_players: Simon Adingra, Amad Diallo and Seko Fofana; Wilfried Zaha left out.
CIV.hinges_on: Taking the chance of a kinder draw than the brutal groups of years past.
notes: Beat France in a World Cup tune-up.
Fae took over mid-AFCON and won it; he infamously lost all 11 games he played for Reading.

ECU.narrative: Sebastian Beccacece's hard-to-beat side finished second in South American qualifying, behind only Argentina.
ECU.key_players: Moises Caicedo and Enner Valencia, behind a formidable back four (Pacho, Hincapie, Estupinan).
ECU.hinges_on: Their stout defense turning goalless grind into deep progress.
notes: Only lost to Brazil and drew Argentina in qualifying.
A genuine sleeper / dark-horse pick.
Had six goalless draws in qualifying (14 scored, 5 conceded in 18 games).

NED.narrative: Ronald Koeman's perennial nearly-men land in arguably the best-balanced group of all.
NED.key_players: Cody Gakpo, Virgil van Dijk and Tijjani Reijnders; Xavi Simons a notable loss.
NED.hinges_on: Settling on a first XI and rhythm despite many under-played squad players.
notes: Best footballing nation never to win it.
Goalkeeper unsettled (Verbruggen likely but inexperienced).
Could go long-ball with Brobbey and Weghorst in desperation.

JPN.narrative: Hajime Moriyasu's side may be the best Asian team ever, cruising through qualifying, but carry a last-16 psychological hoodoo.
JPN.key_players: Kaoru Mitoma (injured, a big blow), Takefusa Kubo, Daizen Maeda and Wataru Endo.
JPN.hinges_on: Finally breaking the round-of-16 ceiling they have hit every time since 2002.
notes: Beat Germany and Spain at the last World Cup.
Injuries (Mitoma, Minamino doubt) may cap their ceiling.
Coach openly says they should aim to win it.

SWE.narrative: Graham Potter, adored in Sweden, has taken over mid-cycle a side that scraped in via the Nations League back door.
SWE.key_players: Alexander Isak and Viktor Gyokeres up front; missing Dejan Kulusevski (injury).
SWE.hinges_on: Whether Potter can get Isak and Gyokeres firing together.
notes: A genuine 'loose horse' with little reliable form to judge.
Gyokeres still not great at holding the ball up.

TUN.narrative: Sabri Lamouchi's Eagles of Carthage qualified without conceding, but from a weak group and with almost no attacking threat.
TUN.key_players: Hannibal Mejbri is the creative hub; Khalil Yari (PSG) a Hail-Mary young forward.
TUN.hinges_on: Finding goals from somewhere; striking options are dire.
notes: Play with extreme caution bordering on paranoia.
Teenager Louis Ben Farhat reportedly withheld by his father.
Changed coach after a poor AFCON.

BEL.narrative: Rudy Garcia's post-golden-generation Belgium have a kind group but lingering questions.
BEL.key_players: Kevin De Bruyne, an aging Romelu Lukaku, Jeremy Doku and Leandro Trossard; Courtois back after a feud.
BEL.hinges_on: Finding a reliable goalscorer and not conceding soft goals.
notes: Scored 29 in eight qualifiers, but mostly against weak sides.
Defense looks less authoritative than the old guard.
De Bruyne in the heat is a question.

EGY.narrative: Hossam Hassan's Egypt settled on a 3-4-1-2 to fit both stars, with this likely Salah's last real World Cup shot.
EGY.key_players: Mohamed Salah and Omar Marmoush up front, knitted together by a creative number 10 in Emam Ashour.
EGY.hinges_on: Whether the coach can make the right in-game calls; doubts persist.
notes: Marmoush eases the load on Salah.
Their veteran keeper is among the oldest players at the tournament.
Coach seen as a motivator more than a tactician.

IRN.narrative: Amir Ghalenoei's experienced, aging Iran chase a first-ever exit from the group amid heavy political tension.
IRN.key_players: Mehdi Taremi; notably without Sardar Azmoun, their best striker, who has criticised the regime.
IRN.hinges_on: Focusing on football despite the geopolitical backdrop.
notes: Staying in Mexico, playing in the US; a possible last-32 meeting with the USA.
Azmoun (57 goals in 91 caps) left out.
An old squad.

NZL.narrative: Cruised through a tiny Oceania qualifying path, barely tested, and arrive boosted by the return of Chris Wood.
NZL.key_players: Chris Wood (talisman), Kosta Barbarouses, Elijah Just and cult hero Tim Payne.
NZL.hinges_on: Scoring goals beyond Wood in a tough group.
notes: Played just five qualifiers (vs ~21 for Iraq).
Tim Payne went from 4,000 to 4.3m Instagram followers after going viral as the 'least-known player'.
Beat Chile 4-1 in a friendly.

ESP.narrative: Euro 2024 winners and many people's favourites, with a stable, balanced squad and a rising teenage star.
ESP.key_players: Lamine Yamal (fitness doubt), Pedri, Rodri, Nico Williams (fitness doubt) and Fabian Ruiz.
ESP.hinges_on: Fitness of Yamal and Nico Williams to provide the direct, wide threat.
notes: First-ever Spain squad with no Real Madrid players.
Lost leadership in Morata and Carvajal; Rodri the lone remaining elder.
Heat suits their possession game; the most 'club-like' team.

CPV.narrative: World Cup debutants and the third-smallest nation ever to qualify, built since 2023 into a tough, all-Europe-based unit.
CPV.key_players: Ryan Mendes (top scorer, 36), keeper Vozinha (39) and Stopira (37), under coach Pedro 'Bubista' Brito.
CPV.hinges_on: Beating Saudi Arabia, the game they will target, to maybe sneak through as a third-placed team.
notes: An aging core getting its one shot.
Roberto 'Pico' Lopes was recruited via a LinkedIn message.
Bubista was 2025 African Coach of the Year.

KSA.narrative: Georgios Donis took over only in April; the squad is almost entirely Saudi Pro League-based.
KSA.key_players: Saud Abdulhamid (on loan at Roma) is the lone overseas player; mostly SPL names.
KSA.hinges_on: Getting out of the group for the first time since 1994.
notes: Famously beat Argentina in 2022.
The foreign influx in the SPL means many players lack game time.
New coach has little time to imprint his ideas.

URU.narrative: Marcelo Bielsa's two-time champions started his reign brightly but have gone off the boil amid a thin generation.
URU.key_players: Darwin Nunez (out of form and rusty), Federico Valverde and Manuel Ugarte.
URU.hinges_on: Nunez rediscovering goals and the squad buying into Bielsa's intensity.
notes: Bielsa's harsh team talks reportedly reduced Nunez to tears (he then scored).
Suarez criticised Bielsa, then offered himself for the World Cup and wasn't picked.
The U20 World Cup winners of 2023 have largely been ignored.

FRA.narrative: Didier Deschamps' final tournament with a frighteningly talented but unbalanced squad before Zidane takes over.
FRA.key_players: Kylian Mbappe, Ousmane Dembele, Desire Doue and Michael Olise; William Saliba a game-one injury doubt.
FRA.hinges_on: Whether a shaky defense and weakened midfield hold up against elite opposition.
notes: An embarrassment of attacking riches and selection headaches.
Midfield leans on a 35-year-old Kante; the back line doesn't fully convince.
Their most fluent recent display was a B-team friendly vs Colombia.

SEN.narrative: Pape Thiaw's Senegal pair a rock-solid defense with experience and exciting youth, the African side most likely to go deep.
SEN.key_players: Sadio Mane, Kalidou Koulibaly and Edouard Mendy, plus teens Lamine Camara and Pape Matar Sarr.
SEN.hinges_on: Starting well against France to top a brutal group.
notes: Conceded just two goals in seven games at AFCON.
Too good to really be called dark horses.
The opener vs France is a 'nice needle' match.

IRQ.narrative: Graham Arnold's side reach a first World Cup in 40 years after an intercontinental playoff win over Bolivia, to jubilation in Baghdad.
IRQ.key_players: Ali Al-Hamadi (ex-AFC Wimbledon) in a well-drilled Arnold team.
IRQ.hinges_on: Nicking a point, and maybe springing a shock in a group where third could fail to advance.
notes: Brutal draw with France, Senegal and Norway.
A win 'would put the cat among the pigeons'.

NOR.narrative: Back at a World Cup after a long absence, top scorers in qualifying having torn Italy apart.
NOR.key_players: Erling Haaland, Martin Odegaard, Alexander Sorloth, Antonio Nusa and Oscar Bobb.
NOR.hinges_on: Whether a lack of tournament know-how derails an otherwise stacked, balanced team.
notes: Beat Sweden in their farewell friendly, relaxed and impressive.
Few precedents for thriving after such a long layoff.
Risk of finishing as a third-placed team in a strong group.

ARG.narrative: Lionel Scaloni's defending champions go with continuity, basically the 2022 final XI four years older, in a pleasant group.
ARG.key_players: Lionel Messi (minutes to be managed), Lautaro Martinez and Julian Alvarez; 38-year-old Otamendi may start.
ARG.hinges_on: Using nous and stability to grind, while easing the load off Messi.
notes: Lots of 'Springsteen-tour' US friendlies, so hard to gauge their true level.
End-of-an-era feel; not the runaway favourites of 2022.
10 of the 11 from the 2022 final remain.

ALG.narrative: Vladimir Petkovic's talented but unpredictable side return, captained by Riyad Mahrez.
ALG.key_players: Riyad Mahrez and a clutch of genuinely strong players.
ALG.hinges_on: Turning quality into results; a real unknown quantity.
notes: Beat the Netherlands in a tune-up.
Must travel ~5,000km across two time zones in three group games, far more than anyone else.

AUT.narrative: Ralf Rangnick's pressing side could bloody a favourite's nose; their Argentina meeting in Dallas has epic potential.
AUT.key_players: Marcel Sabitzer, Konrad Laimer and Marko Arnautovic, plus new declarees Paul Wanner and Carney Chukwuemeka.
AUT.hinges_on: Adapting their high-energy press to the heat.
notes: Wanner and Chukwuemeka switched from the German and English youth setups.
Heavily reliant on Arnautovic for goals.

JOR.narrative: A first-ever World Cup for Jamal Sellami's Jordan, who shone at the recent Arab Cup.
JOR.key_players: Mousa Al-Tamari among the few names familiar to European audiences.
JOR.hinges_on: Springing a surprise as an unknown quantity.
notes: Beat Saudi Arabia and Egypt en route to the Arab Cup final, losing only to Morocco in extra time.
Could surprise people.

POR.narrative: Roberto Martinez's Portugal carry arguably the most complete, balanced squad in the tournament, overshadowed by the Ronaldo question.
POR.key_players: Bruno Fernandes, Vitinha, Joao Neves, Rafael Leao, Bernardo Silva and Nuno Mendes; plus Cristiano Ronaldo.
POR.hinges_on: Whether Ronaldo accepts a reduced role, and conflict-averse Martinez manages it.
notes: Ronaldo scored in both Nations League finals wins (and even pressed).
Open question of who starts up top if not Ronaldo (Goncalo Ramos, Joao Felix).
An insanely deep midfield.

COD.narrative: Sebastien Desabre's side reach a first World Cup in 52 years, tactically disciplined and mostly European-born.
COD.key_players: Yoane Wissa (a poor season at Newcastle), Axel Tuanzebe and Arthur Masuaku.
COD.hinges_on: Getting a result against Colombia in game two.
notes: A massive country living through constant conflict.
Could create chaos in an open group.

UZB.narrative: Fabio Cannavaro's Uzbekistan reach a maiden World Cup after a history of near-misses.
UZB.key_players: Abdukodir Khusanov (Man City defender), their best-known player.
UZB.hinges_on: Grinding out draws and nicking goals on the break.
notes: Cannavaro was appointed after they had already qualified.
Tipped by the panel to finish bottom of the group.

COL.narrative: Nestor Lorenzo's Colombia were on a brilliant run to the 2024 Copa final but have lost their way since.
COL.key_players: James Rodriguez (34, may retire after) and Luis Diaz; Jhon Duran omitted.
COL.hinges_on: Rediscovering identity and form around an aging core.
notes: Only two players under 25, a very old squad.
Were dismantled by a second-string France.
Could underperform expectations.

ENG.narrative: Thomas Tuchel's settled, tournament-calibrated side carry a real sense of purpose and arrive sharp and fresh.
ENG.key_players: Harry Kane (astonishing form), Declan Rice, Jude Bellingham, Elliot Anderson and Morgan Rogers.
ENG.hinges_on: Overcoming the neurosis and circus; they look to have all the other pieces.
notes: Had the highest possession in European qualifying, crucial in the heat.
Tuchel ruthlessly cut Foden and Cole Palmer; Ivan Toney, Dan Burn and Henderson are squad options.
Coach unbothered by the English media sideshow.

CRO.narrative: 2018 runners-up and 2022 third place, Zlatko Dalic's golden generation makes one last run.
CRO.key_players: Luka Modric (closing on 200 caps), Mateo Kovacic and Ivan Perisic; youth in Luka Vuskovic and Martin Baturina.
CRO.hinges_on: How long the aging core lasts in the heat.
notes: Modric, Perisic and Kovacic have 462 caps between them.
Likely scrapping with Ghana for second.

GHA.narrative: Carlos Queiroz's Black Stars arrive in poor form, their reputation outstripping the current squad.
GHA.key_players: Mohammed Kudus, Thomas Partey and Jordan Ayew.
GHA.hinges_on: A lot more than the panel expects; they look unbalanced and out of sorts.
notes: Hammered by Germany and Mexico recently.
Pundits skeptical Ghana are the 'superpower' their name implies.

PAN.narrative: Thomas Christiansen's Panama, at a second World Cup, shouldn't be the whipping boys people expect.
PAN.key_players: Adalberto Carrasquilla and Ismael Diaz in an experienced CONCACAF core.
PAN.hinges_on: Causing an upset, starting with Ghana in Toronto.
notes: Escaped a Copa America group with Uruguay and the USA.
Reached the CONCACAF Nations League final.
